text: The robotic system is docked, and the surgeon visualizes the ventral hernia located at the umbilicus, with three 8mm trocars placed in the left lateral abdomen.

The surgeon initiates dissection into the preperitoneal space, starting cephalad and moving caudad. The central preperitoneal fat pad is developed toward the hernia sac at the umbilicus, preparing the space for mesh placement.

The surgeon reduces the hernia sac at the umbilicus, carefully dissecting it from the overlying umbilical stalk. The dissection extends toward the right lateral side of the abdominal wall, transitioning from the preperitoneal fat pad to the thin lateral peritoneal layer, ensuring the integrity of this layer for proper mesh placement.

The surgeon completes circumferential dissection to create a large pocket for synthetic mesh placement, ensuring sufficient overlap to minimize recurrence. A fascial closure suture is introduced, and a ruler measures the defect size at approximately 2.5 cm by 2 cm. The fascia is closed using a PDS absorbable unidirectional barbed suture, maintaining tension during closure.

The surgeon removes the fascial closure suture and introduces sutures for anchoring the mesh and closing the peritoneum. A polyester ProGrip mesh, cut to a thirteen-centimeter diameter circle, is placed over the hernia defect with wide overlap. The mesh is anchored at the three, six, nine, and twelve o'clock positions with Vicryl sutures, with an additional suture in the center.

The surgeon closes the peritoneal flap using a unidirectional barbed suture, ensuring the mesh is completely excluded from the abdominal cavity. Any holes in the peritoneum, including one within the reduced hernia sac, are closed with interrupted vicryl sutures. The omentum is repositioned over the viscera, and all needles and sutures are removed from the abdominal cavity.
